title: Charging your EV from solar panels
description: Can you charge an electric car from home solar? Real UK numbers: miles per year from a 4kW system, the kit you need, and when cheap night rates beat solar.
blurb: How many miles a 4kW solar array really puts in your EV, and when a cheap night tariff quietly beats it.
lede: On paper it is the perfect match: panels on the roof making electricity, and a car on the drive that runs on it. Plenty of UK households now have both, and the appeal of driving on sunshine is obvious. The reality is more interesting than the sales pitch. A typical 4kW array genuinely can put thousands of miles into an EV each year, but only if the car is at home while the sun is out, and only if solar charging actually beats the very cheap overnight rates that EV tariffs now offer. Sometimes it does. Sometimes, surprisingly, it does not. This guide works through the real numbers so you can tell which side of the line you are on.
short_answer: Yes, a home solar system can charge an EV, and a 4kW array's surplus can realistically supply 1,800 to 2,400 kWh a year, enough for roughly 5,500 to 7,500 miles if you capture most of it. Driving on diverted solar effectively costs only the export payment you give up, typically 5 to 15p per kWh, so as little as 1.5p a mile. The catch: if your export rate is higher than your off-peak EV tariff rate, you come out ahead by exporting the solar and charging the car at 5.5 to 9p overnight instead.

sections.h2: How charging from solar actually works
sections.html:
First, a common misconception to clear up. Your panels do not plug into the car. A standard grid-tied solar system feeds AC power into your house wiring, the house uses what it needs, and the rest flows out to the grid. Charging from solar simply means timing the car's charging so it soaks up that surplus before it leaves the building. The electrons are interchangeable; what matters is the metering.

There are three practical ways to do it:

  • The crude way: plug in a three-pin granny charger around midday. It draws a steady 2.3kW, so on a bright day most of that comes from your panels and the shortfall tops up from the grid. Zero extra kit, but imprecise, and the usual caveats about three-pin charging versus a proper wallbox apply.
  • The proper way: a smart wallbox with a solar or surplus-matching mode. A clamp on your meter tail measures export in real time and the charger continuously adjusts its draw to match, so the car takes only what would otherwise be exported. Most major wallbox ranges now offer this as a standard feature rather than a premium one.
  • The buffered way: a home battery stores the daytime surplus and releases it whenever the car plugs in, which solves the car-must-be-home-at-noon problem at the cost of a much bigger outlay.

One technical limit shapes all of this. The EV charging standard sets a minimum charge rate of 6 amps, about 1.4kW on a single-phase UK supply. If your surplus is below that, a solar-only mode either pauses or blends in some grid power. On grey days a 4kW array spends a lot of time under that threshold, which is why immersion-heater diverters, which can use any trickle of surplus, behave differently from car chargers. Our guide to solar diverters covers that distinction in detail.

sections.h2: How many miles a 4kW system really gives you
sections.html:
A 4kW system in the UK generates roughly 3,400 to 4,000 kWh a year, and a typical installed price is £7,000 to £8,500. But you cannot send all of it to the car. The house takes its share first, generation is heavily skewed toward summer, and the car has to be plugged in while the surplus exists. Here is what that looks like in practice for a south-facing 4kW system in central England, assuming an EV that manages 3.5 miles per kWh and about 10 per cent charging losses:

Time of yearTypical daily generationRealistic surplus for the carMiles added per day
Sunny day, June or July16 to 20 kWh10 to 13 kWh30 to 40
Bright day, April or September10 to 14 kWh6 to 9 kWh20 to 28
Overcast day, December or January1 to 3 kWh0 to 1 kWh0 to 3
Whole year3,400 to 4,000 kWh1,800 to 2,400 kWh5,500 to 7,500 miles

Generation figures for a south-facing 4kW system in central England. Miles assume 3.5 miles per kWh at the battery and 10 per cent charging losses; see our guide to EV miles per kWh for where your car sits.

Two things jump out of that table. First, the annual potential is genuinely large: the average UK car covers about 7,000 miles a year, so a 4kW array's surplus can in principle cover most of a typical car's mileage. Second, the seasonal spread is brutal. Roughly 70 per cent of your generation lands between April and September. In December, solar charging is close to a rounding error, and you will be leaning on your tariff for almost everything.

sections.h2: Solar versus a cheap night tariff: the sum most guides skip
sections.html:
Here is the part the glossy brochures leave out. Solar electricity sent to the car is not free, because every kWh you divert is a kWh you could have exported for payment under the Smart Export Guarantee. The true cost of solar charging is your export rate. And since EV tariffs now offer overnight import rates as low as 5.5 to 9p per kWh, the comparison is closer than you might expect.

The decision rule is one line: charge from solar when your export rate is below your cheapest import rate; otherwise export the solar and charge overnight. If you receive 5p per kWh for export and pay 7p off-peak, solar charging wins, narrowly. If you receive a 15p export rate and pay 5.5p overnight on a smart EV tariff, every solar kWh you push into the car costs you 9.5p versus the alternative, and you would be almost three times better off exporting the lot and filling the battery at night.

Here is what 8,000 miles a year costs from each source. At 3.5 miles per kWh that needs about 2,290 kWh in the battery, or roughly 2,500 kWh from the meter once home charging losses are included:

How you chargeEffective rateCost of 8,000 miles a year
Solar surplus, giving up a 5p export rate5p per kWh£125
Smart EV tariff overnight5.5p per kWh£138
Typical EV off-peak window7p per kWh£175
Solar surplus, giving up a 15p export rate15p per kWh£375
Standard variable rate26.11p per kWh£653
Public rapid charging79p per kWh£1,975
Petrol car at 45mpg, for comparison159p per litre£1,285

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Off-peak EV rates of 5.5 to 9p, public rapid average of 79p and petrol at 159p per litre are mid-2026 figures; export rates under the Smart Export Guarantee currently span roughly 5 to 15p. Check your own tariff and export rate before deciding.

Notice how tight the top of that table is. The honest conclusion is that solar charging and a good night tariff both get you to around 1.5 to 2.5p a mile, and the difference between them over a year is a few tens of pounds, not hundreds. Either one beats the standard rate by roughly £500 a year and petrol by over £1,100. Getting off the standard rate matters far more than the solar question; our guide to the best time to charge an electric car covers how to pick your windows.

sections.h2: The kit you need, and what it costs
sections.html:
You do not need much beyond what a solar home and an EV home already have:

  • A smart wallbox with a surplus-matching mode. Expect roughly £800 to £1,200 installed, about the same as any quality 7kW charger, since solar matching is now a software feature rather than special hardware. Look for a charger that takes a CT clamp reading of your export and offers a pure-solar mode plus a blended mode that tops up with grid power below the 1.4kW threshold.
  • Compatibility with your tariff. If you are on a smart EV tariff that controls charging times, check the charger and tariff work together. Some smart tariffs require control via the charger or the car, and you want solar matching by day and cheap-rate charging by night from the same box without fighting itself.
  • Optionally, a home battery. Useful if the car is rarely home in daylight, but a battery sized to time-shift meaningful car charging is a £4,000-plus purchase that needs its own justification. Run that as a separate decision; the maths in our guide to whether solar is worth it applies equally to adding storage.

If you already have an ordinary dumb wallbox, you are not stuck. Charging on a timer through the middle of the day captures a decent fraction of your surplus in summer, and at 7kW the car will simply draw the balance from the grid. Whether that balance is cheap depends on your daytime rate, which is why people on agile or tracker-style pricing sometimes find sunny summer middays cost very little anyway.

sections.h2: Practical limits, and how to plan around them
sections.html:
A few realities decide whether solar charging works for your household rather than in a spreadsheet:

  • The car has to be home in daylight. This is the big one. If the car leaves at 8am and returns at 6pm five days a week, your weekday surplus is gone before it returns, and you are realistically capturing weekend sunshine only, perhaps a third of the annual surplus. Home workers, retirees and two-car households where one car stays home are the natural fit.
  • Winter barely counts. Plan on solar covering meaningful mileage from roughly March to October and almost nothing in the darkest three months. Your overnight tariff is the backbone; solar is the summer bonus.
  • House demand comes first. Surplus-matching chargers only take what the house is not using, which is exactly what you want, but it means a washing machine or oven running at noon directly slows the car.
  • Size the array generously if you are installing now. If you are buying solar with an EV already on the drive, the marginal cost of extra panels is small while the scaffolding is up. A 5 to 6kW array changes the charging maths considerably more than any charger feature does.

It is also worth checking your charge target. Routine charging to 80 per cent rather than 100 leaves headroom to absorb a sunny afternoon when it arrives, instead of finding the battery already full from last night's cheap window.

Charging an EV from a 4kW solar system is real, not a gimmick: captured well, the surplus is worth 5,500 to 7,500 miles a year at around 1.5 to 4p a mile, with the strongest months from late spring to early autumn. But it is an optimisation, not a revolution, because smart EV tariffs already deliver 1.6 to 2.5p a mile overnight all year round. Run the one-line test: if your export rate is lower than your cheapest import rate, divert solar into the car; if it is higher, export the sunshine, bank the payment, and charge at night. Either way you should be paying a small fraction of what petrol or the standard rate costs, and our guide to EV charging at home costs has the full method for working out your own per-mile figure.

faq.q: Can solar panels charge an electric car directly, without the grid?
faq.a: Not in a normal grid-tied setup. The panels feed your house wiring through an inverter, and the car charger draws from the house like any other appliance. A surplus-matching smart charger adjusts its draw in real time so the car takes only the power that would otherwise be exported, which gives the same financial result as a direct connection.

faq.q: How many solar panels do I need to charge an electric car?
faq.a: A 4kW system, around 10 panels, generates 3,400 to 4,000 kWh a year in the UK. After the house takes its share, the surplus of roughly 1,800 to 2,400 kWh can cover 5,500 to 7,500 miles, close to the average UK car's annual mileage. If you are installing solar with an EV in mind, a 5 to 6kW array gives much more comfortable headroom.

faq.q: Is it better to export my solar and charge the car at night instead?
faq.a: Sometimes, yes. Every solar kWh you put in the car forfeits the export payment, so solar charging effectively costs your export rate. If you export at 15p but can charge overnight at 5.5 to 7p on an EV tariff, exporting and night-charging wins clearly. If your export rate is below your cheapest import rate, divert the solar into the car.

faq.q: Can I charge my EV from solar in winter?
faq.a: Barely. A 4kW system might generate only 1 to 3 kWh on an overcast December day, often below the 1.4kW minimum rate an EV charger can use, and the house consumes most of it. Treat solar charging as a March to October benefit and rely on a cheap overnight tariff through the winter.

faq.q: Do I need a special charger to charge from solar?
faq.a: For best results, yes: a smart wallbox with a solar or surplus-matching mode, typically £800 to £1,200 installed, which is similar to any quality 7kW charger. It uses a meter clamp to track your export and matches the car's draw to it. Without one, simply timing charging for sunny middays still captures a worthwhile share in summer.
